summary: Small cleanup PR for ftSandbag motion definitions. It replaced anonymous numeric motion-state fields and address-based callback names with semantic fighter motion-state constants and callback names, while keeping the same behavior. The PR touched only ftSb_Init.c and ftSb_Init.h and had no recorded review discussion.
what_changed: Added an include for `ft/forward.h` before `ftSb_Init.h` in `ftSb_Init.c`.

Updated `ftSb_Init_MotionStateTable` entry 295 to use named values instead of raw numeric literals: `FtStateChange_None` instead of `0`, and `(FtMoveId_Unk01 << 24) | (1 << 22)` instead of `0x01400000`.

Renamed the four Sandbag motion-state callbacks from address-derived names to motion-state-role names: `ftSb_Init_8014FC20` to `ftSb_MS_341_Anim`, `ftSb_Init_8014FC24` to `ftSb_MS_341_IASA`, `ftSb_Init_8014FC28` to `ftSb_MS_341_Phys`, and `ftSb_Init_8014FC48` to `ftSb_MS_341_Coll`.
Updated the corresponding declarations in `ftSb_Init.h` while preserving the address comments `14FC20`, `14FC24`, `14FC28`, and `14FC48`.
Behavior appears unchanged: the Anim and IASA callbacks remain empty, Phys still calls `ft_80084F3C(gobj)`, and Coll still calls `ft_80083F88(gobj)`.
smart_moves: Converted raw motion-state flags into named bit construction, making the table easier to audit against fighter state-change and move-id conventions.
Used the `ftSb_MS_341_<Role>` naming pattern to encode that these functions are motion-state callbacks and to identify their callback role: Anim, IASA, Phys, and Coll.
Kept address comments in the header while improving symbol names, preserving reverse-engineering traceability without retaining unhelpful address-based function names.
Made a focused, low-risk cleanup with only two files and no functional changes evident from the diff.
decomp_lessons: For fighter motion tables, prefer semantic constants such as `FtStateChange_None` and `FtMoveId_*` expressions over raw hex or decimal literals when the meaning is known.
Motion-state callbacks are clearer when named by fighter, motion-state identifier, and callback slot, e.g. `ftSb_MS_341_Anim`, `ftSb_MS_341_IASA`, `ftSb_MS_341_Phys`, and `ftSb_MS_341_Coll`.
Retaining address comments in headers can preserve mapping information after renaming functions to meaningful decomp names.
A cleanup can improve long-term maintainability even when it does not change matching behavior, especially by replacing autogenerated address names with table-aware names.
naming_conventions: `ftSb_MS_341_Anim` for the animation callback of Sandbag motion state 341.
`ftSb_MS_341_IASA` for the IASA callback of Sandbag motion state 341.
`ftSb_MS_341_Phys` for the physics callback of Sandbag motion state 341.
`ftSb_MS_341_Coll` for the collision callback of Sandbag motion state 341.
Use `ftSb_Init_*` for initialization-related functions, but use `ftSb_MS_*` for motion-state callback functions when the function is tied to a MotionState table entry.
Keep hexadecimal/address comments such as `/* 14FC20 */` in declarations even when the symbol name is improved.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replacing `0x01400000` with `(FtMoveId_Unk01 << 24) | (1 << 22)` documents the packed bit layout while preserving the same value.
Replacing `0` with `FtStateChange_None` documents a known enum meaning without changing the table data.
Renaming no-op callbacks and callbacks that forward to existing helper functions can be done safely when all references in the motion table and header are updated together.
follow_up_queries: Find other fighter MotionState tables still using raw `0` or raw hex flag literals.
Find other address-derived motion-state callback names that could be renamed to the `ft<fighter>_MS_<state>_<Anim|IASA|Phys|Coll>` pattern.
Check whether motion state 341 has a more descriptive state name than numeric `341` or `FtMoveId_Unk01` in later decomp work.
